metric_value: Three EMC goals: (a) align EMC with prospect business issues; (b) align prospect executives with EMC solutions; (c) pull through storage solutions via services.
EMC has a strong technology-first brand but is not well known for either business insight or services.
Strong brand recognition within CIO's team but limited access to the CIO; sales executives lack business-level insight to be credible with LOB executives.
Top policy driver: Mandate to lower overall costs.
Driver 2: Consolidation within the datacenter.
Driver 3: Improving business value of IT.
Driver 4: Consolidation of datacenters.
Driver 5: Changing data retention policies to retain more information.
Driver 6: Staging data on devices based on an information lifecycle plan.
Driver 7: Regulation or compliance.
Top acquisition-importance attributes: improved backup/restore times; better I/O throughput per drive; improved MTBF; lower cost per GB; improved TCO.
3/4 of respondents moderately-to-very familiar with the concept of Pools of Storage.
But market is not yet buying in pools.
1/3 of respondents believe they have no fixed-content or static data — a drag on the midline pool.
ILM is a classic 'Great New Idea' with all the challenges and opportunities.
ILM Timeframe: the next decade (analogous to systems management in the 1990s).

ILM (Information Lifecycle Management) as a formal product category emerged around 2003-2006 with EMC, IBM, and HP offering tiered storage solutions. The timeframe for mainstream ILM adoption was approximately 2003-2007, broadly consistent with predictions in this study. Automated storage tiering became a standard feature of enterprise storage arrays by mid-2000s.
notes: Placeholder. | ILM as a storage category was realized within the predicted timeframe; tiered storage and policy-based data management became standard by 2006-2007. Source: https://cacm.acm.org/research/information-life-cycle-management/

metric_value: ILM will come to be the big storage-related market battle of the decade — whether called ILM or not.

The 'decade-long battle' for ILM leadership was won not by a single vendor but by cloud storage economics. EMC, IBM, and HP competed through the 2000s, but by 2010-2015 cloud object storage (AWS S3, Azure Blob) disrupted traditional tiered storage ILM frameworks. The battle outcome was ultimately displacement rather than vendor consolidation.
notes: Placeholder. | ILM vendor competition occurred as predicted through mid-2000s, but cloud disruption changed the competitive landscape by 2010. Source: General storage industry history.

metric_value: Winners: None yet. Opening gun just went off. EMC making waves.
ILM definition is too vague, but vagueness gives ability to morph the concept over time as products mature.
Big concept short on solutions — no one can deliver on the ILM vision anytime soon.
ILM looks like 'more IT infrastructure' with few tangible business benefits to non-IT execs — likely cause of impeded growth in ILM market.
Immediate services opportunity around ILM despite long product roadmap.
Info access & storage requirements accelerate; new data capture & storage costs create significant budget trade-offs.
